settings.title: Brushing Between Grooms, the Right Way
settings.excerpt: Most of the painful matting we have to clip out started as a coat that looked fine on top but was felting underneath.
settings.body:
Owners are often shocked when we find matting on a dog that looked perfectly brushed. The trouble hides where the surface brush never reaches.

A coat can look glossy and tidy on top while felting quietly underneath, like a neat lawn over tangled roots. By the time it shows on the surface, the mat is usually well established.

Where mats really form

Mats form first at the friction points: behind the ears, in the armpits, and where the collar sits. A brush dragged over the top coat skips straight past them.

The usual hiding spots, in case you want to check today:

  • Behind and under the ears.
  • The armpits and the chest where legs meet body.
  • Around the collar and harness lines.

Brush to the skin

Brush right down to the skin. Part the coat in sections and work from the skin outward, paying special attention to those hidden spots. A slicker brush and a metal comb together do the job, with the comb confirming what the brush only seems to.

Five minutes every couple of days beats a long, uncomfortable de-matting session for your dog later. Tight mats pull on the skin and sometimes cannot be brushed out at all, which means clipping them off and starting the coat again.

And it keeps our visits a pleasure rather than a chore. Bring your brush to the next appointment and we will happily show you the technique on your own dog.
